title: practical english usage - Part 249
content:
A  fall-rise intonation suggests that something is incomplete, or uncertain, or that c   fall-rise Are you staying or going?

and falls on the second part.

In 'alternative questions’  with or, the voice rises on the first part of the question Did he sign it?

Is that the secretary?

grammar *315   pronunciation: weak and strong forms has the vowel /ə/   or no vowel; a few weak forms are pronounced with /ɪ/.

stress. So the unstressed ('weak') pronunciation is the normal one. This usually are generally found together with other more important words which  carry the articles and auxiliary verbs. Such words are not usually stressed, because they Most words with two pronunciations are prepositions, pronouns, conjunctions, 2 stressed      or  not?

I'm looking at /ət/  you.  What are you looking at /æt /?

pronunciations: one is used when they are not stressed, and the other when Some English words - for example at, for, have, and, us - have two 1 What    are   weak     and   strong     forms?

315  pronunciation:         weak    and   strong     forms For intonation in question tags, ►    305.

'I'd like to play tennis! 'So, what's the problem?' 'There's no problem!

than is said.

A  fall-rise in the wrong place can be misunderstood as suggesting more 'Can I have some more coffee?' 'At once, Your Majesty!

A  falling intonation can also turn a polite request into an order.

"That's our train?' 'Is it?' 'No, I'm asking you!

misunderstood as a statement.

If a declarative question (►  302) is made on a falling intonation, it may be 'That's our train.'  7 don't know! 'Yes, it is, I'm telling you!

as a question.

If a statement is made on a rising intonation, it may be misunderstood 4 intonation        and   misunderstandings Speech and Spoken Exchanges   Section 27 grammar •  315   pronunciation: weak and
